metadata.title: FIRE Retirement Simulator
metadata.description: Monte Carlo simulation-based retirement planning tool

common.successRateHelp: Percentage of simulations where portfolio survives entire retirement
common.fundedRatioHelp: Weighted ratio of actual vs target spending, reflecting real living standard in retirement

sidebar.dataSourceJstDesc: Jordà-Schularick-Taylor Macrohistory Database (2017), stock/bond returns and inflation across 16 developed countries; 2021-2025 extended with IMF/OECD/yfinance data
sidebar.dataSourceFireDesc: Long-term US market data curated by the Bogleheads community (Simba's Backtesting Spreadsheet): S&P 500 (incl. predecessors), MSCI EAFE (post-1970), 10Y US Treasury
sidebar.poolingEqualHelp: Each country sampled with equal probability
sidebar.poolingGdpSqrtHelp: Weighted by sqrt(GDP) — balances economic representativeness and diversity
sidebar.decliningRateHelp: Annual real spending decline after start age (Hurd & Rohwedder 2022: 1.7-2.4%)
sidebar.decliningStartAgeHelp: Age when spending begins to decline. Well-supported by research for 65+; FIRE retirees typically maintain stable spending before this age
sidebar.glidePathHelp: Linearly transition asset allocation over time from current to target during retirement
sidebar.borrowingCostHelp: Leverage borrowing cost = short-term rate ≈ inflation + spread

allocation.title: 🎯 Allocation Optimizer
allocation.nearOptimalTooltip: Allocations with funded ratio within 1% of the best. These perform statistically similarly — pick based on your preference.
allocation.heatmapHint: Gold borders = near-optimal zone. Star = best allocation.
allocation.paretoHint: Blue diamonds show the best trade-off between safety (funded ratio) and upside (median final). No other allocation beats them on both.

dashboard.title: FIRE Dashboard
dashboard.subtitle: Comprehensive retirement plan assessment at a glance
dashboard.scoreDescription: Weighted score combining success rate, funded ratio, and safe withdrawal rate
dashboard.insightGreat: Congratulations! Your retirement plan is very robust with a high overall readiness score.
dashboard.insightGood: Plan looks good overall, but there's room for optimization. Consider adjusting withdrawal or allocation.
dashboard.insightCaution: Plan carries some risk. Consider reducing annual withdrawal, increasing savings, or adjusting strategy.
dashboard.insightDanger: Plan carries high risk. Strongly recommend reducing withdrawal amount or extending working years.

methodology.title: Methodology
methodology.intro: FIRE Lab uses Monte Carlo simulation with Block Bootstrap resampling of historical market data. This page explains the core methods, data sources, and assumptions behind the simulator.
methodology.bootstrapP1: Instead of assuming returns follow a specific distribution (e.g. normal), we draw directly from historical data. The Block Bootstrap preserves the autocorrelation and cross-asset correlations observed in real markets by sampling contiguous blocks of historical returns.
methodology.bootstrapP2: For each simulation path, the algorithm repeatedly draws a random block size (from a configurable range), picks a random starting year from the historical dataset, and copies that many consecutive years of returns. Indices wrap around circularly to avoid boundary bias. This process repeats until the full retirement horizon is filled.
methodology.bootstrapP3: In pooled multi-country mode, each block also randomly selects a country (weighted by square root of GDP by default), mixing return regimes across 16 developed economies within a single path.
methodology.mcP1: The simulator runs thousands of independent retirement paths (default varies by server capacity). Each path represents one possible sequence of market returns over your retirement horizon.
methodology.mcP2: For each year of each path: (1) portfolio grows by the sampled real return, (2) annual withdrawal is deducted, (3) custom cash flows (expenses, then income) are applied, (4) if the portfolio reaches zero, the path is marked as depleted. Results are aggregated into percentile bands (P5 through P95) to show the range of possible outcomes.
methodology.successP1: The percentage of simulation paths where the portfolio survives the entire retirement period. A path that depletes in the final year is counted as successful (it funded the full horizon). This metric answers: "What is the probability my money lasts?"
methodology.fundedP1: The average fraction of the retirement horizon that is funded across all paths. A path depleting at year 15 of a 30-year retirement contributes 0.5. Unlike success rate, funded ratio captures partial outcomes — a plan with 80% success rate but early depletion in failed paths scores lower than one where failures occur late.
methodology.guardrailP1: The guardrail strategy dynamically adjusts withdrawals based on the current probability of portfolio survival, without running nested simulations at each step.
methodology.guardrailP2: Before simulation, a lookup table maps (withdrawal rate, remaining years) to survival probability. During simulation, the current success rate is checked each year. If it drops below the lower guardrail, spending is cut. If it rises above the upper guardrail, spending is increased. Between the bounds, no change is made.
methodology.guardrailP3: This approach allows real-time spending adjustments that respond to market conditions while maintaining a target success probability throughout retirement.
methodology.dataP1JST: Jordà-Schularick-Taylor (JST) Macrohistory Database — stock returns, bond returns, and inflation for 16 developed countries from 1871 to 2020. Years 2021-2025 are extended using IMF World Economic Outlook, OECD Main Economic Indicators, and Yahoo Finance data.
methodology.dataP2Fire: Bogleheads community Simba's Backtesting Spreadsheet — long-term US market data including S&P 500 (and predecessors), MSCI EAFE (post-1970), and 10-Year US Treasury returns.
methodology.dataP3: All returns used in simulation are real (inflation-adjusted). The simulator supports both single-country analysis and pooled multi-country sampling.
methodology.countriesP1: Australia, Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, Netherlands, Norway, Portugal, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, United Kingdom, and United States.
methodology.limitP1: Historical data is not a guarantee of future performance. The Block Bootstrap preserves historical return distributions but cannot predict structural changes in markets.
methodology.limitP2: The simulator assumes a constant asset allocation (or a predetermined glide path) and does not model behavioral responses to market conditions beyond the guardrail rules.
methodology.limitP3: Tax effects, currency risk for international investors, and transaction costs beyond expense ratios are not modeled. Results should be used for planning purposes, not as financial advice.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: Common questions about retirement simulation, withdrawal strategies, and how to use FIRE Lab.
faq.q1: What is the 4% rule, and is it still valid?
faq.a1: The 4% rule (Bengen 1994) suggests withdrawing 4% of your initial portfolio annually, adjusted for inflation. It was derived from US historical data and targets a 30-year retirement. Our simulator lets you test this — and any other rate — against 150+ years of multi-country data. Under global pooled data, the safe rate for 95% success is typically closer to 3.2-3.5%.
faq.q2: What is Monte Carlo simulation, and why use it for retirement planning?
faq.a2: Monte Carlo simulation generates thousands of possible retirement scenarios by randomly sampling from historical market returns. Unlike a single projection using average returns, it shows the full range of possible outcomes and the probability of your plan succeeding under different market conditions.
faq.q3: What is Block Bootstrap, and how is it different from random sampling?
faq.a3: Block Bootstrap samples contiguous blocks of historical returns (e.g., 3-7 consecutive years at a time) rather than individual years. This preserves the year-to-year momentum, volatility clustering, and cross-asset correlations that exist in real markets. Pure random sampling would destroy these patterns and underestimate the risk of prolonged downturns.
faq.q4: What is a guardrail withdrawal strategy?
faq.a4: A guardrail strategy sets upper and lower bounds on your portfolio's estimated success rate. When markets perform well and success rises above the upper guardrail, you increase spending. When markets decline and success falls below the lower guardrail, you cut spending. This dynamic approach typically allows 15-25% higher initial withdrawals compared to a fixed strategy at the same risk level.
faq.q5: What is funded ratio, and how is it different from success rate?
faq.a5: Success rate is binary — did the portfolio last the full retirement, yes or no. Funded ratio is the average fraction of retirement years that were funded. A plan with 80% success rate could have failed paths depleting at year 5 or year 28 — funded ratio captures this difference. A higher funded ratio means failures, when they occur, happen later.
faq.q6: Should I use US-only data or global pooled data?
faq.a6: US-only data reflects a single country's exceptional historical performance and may overestimate future returns. Global pooled data (16 countries, GDP-weighted) provides a more conservative and arguably more robust baseline, especially for investors with internationally diversified portfolios. We recommend global pooled data for primary planning and US data as an optimistic scenario.
faq.q7: How many simulations should I run?
faq.a7: More simulations produce more stable results. 2,000-5,000 is sufficient for most analyses. The server automatically recommends a count based on available computing resources. For sensitivity analysis or asset allocation scans, fewer simulations per combination are used to keep total runtime manageable.
faq.q8: What does the spending decline (EBRI) strategy model?
faq.a8: Research (Hurd & Rohwedder 2022) shows that real spending typically declines 1.7-2.4% annually after age 65 as retirees naturally reduce consumption. The spending decline strategy models this pattern, allowing a higher initial withdrawal rate since future spending needs decrease over time.
faq.q9: How accurate is the buy vs rent calculator?
faq.a9: The calculator models the major financial factors: mortgage payments, property taxes, maintenance, home appreciation, rent growth, and investment returns for the renter. It uses historical data distributions when available. However, it does not model local tax benefits (mortgage interest deduction), rental market constraints, or non-financial factors like stability and flexibility.
faq.a10: No. FIRE Lab is an educational tool for exploring retirement scenarios. Historical returns do not guarantee future performance. The simulator cannot account for your complete financial situation, tax circumstances, or personal risk tolerance. Consult a qualified financial advisor for personalized advice.

simulator.intro: Run thousands of retirement scenarios using 150+ years of historical market data from 16 countries. Test fixed and dynamic withdrawal strategies, custom cash flows, and asset allocation — powered by Block Bootstrap sampling for realistic sequence-of-returns modeling.

sensitivity.intro: Find the withdrawal rate that matches your target success rate or funded ratio. Visualize how changing your annual withdrawal affects retirement outcomes across thousands of Monte Carlo simulations.

guardrail.intro: Simulate dynamic spending rules that automatically adjust withdrawals based on portfolio performance. Compare guardrail strategies against fixed withdrawal baselines with Monte Carlo analysis and historical backtesting across 150+ years of data.

allocation.intro: Scan hundreds of domestic stock, international stock, and bond combinations to find the optimal asset allocation for your retirement portfolio. View results as heatmaps, ternary plots, and Pareto frontiers.

buyVsRent.intro: Compare the long-term financial outcomes of buying versus renting a home. Includes both deterministic analysis and Monte Carlo simulation with historical market data for home prices, rents, mortgage rates, and investment returns.

accumulation.intro: Calculate when you can achieve financial independence based on your income, expenses, and savings rate. Uses Monte Carlo simulation with dynamic safe withdrawal rates to find your FIRE age across thousands of market scenarios.

footer.disclaimer: For educational and informational purposes only. Not financial advice. Past performance does not guarantee future results.
footer.dataAttribution: Data: JST Macrohistory Database (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0); US market data from the Bogleheads community Simba's Backtesting Spreadsheet. 2021-2025 JST extension uses IMF WEO, OECD MEI, and yfinance.
